EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations around a quarter inch or a little more in Union County in extreme southeast South Dakota. Travel and walking were made extremely hazardous, and power lines and trees were damaged. Lesser ice accumulations were reported to the north and west, to the Sioux Falls and Yankton areas, producing slippery conditions in those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle, mixed at times with snow, produced a light glazing of ice, making driving and walking surfaces very slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 1 to 4 inches combined with deep existing snow  cover and strong northwest winds to produce blowing and drifting snow. The snow began on Saturday February 13th, while the strong winds and blowing snow were observed on Sunday February 14th into the morning of February 15th. The blowing and drifting snow and resulting accidents closed parts of Interstates 90 and 29 for a period, and several accidents were reported on other roads as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 1 to 3 inches, beginning early on February 13th, and existing deep snow cover, combined with strong northwest winds which developed on February 14th, to produce blowing and drifting snow which reduced visibilities in some areas. The developing slick roads resulted in several accidents, which continued after the snow had ended and visibilities had improved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River which began in March due to snowmelt continued through all of April, with the river falling slowly during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy snow cover caused major flooding of the Big Sioux River beginning on March 12th. Flooding decreased late in the month, ending along part of the river, but continuing past the end of March in other places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack caused flooding of the Big Sioux River which resulted in moderate flooding. Several fields and roads were flooded. The river crested at around 3 feet above flood stage near Dell Rapids on March 176th, and at 3.4 feet above flood stage at North Cliff Avenue in Sioux Falls on the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Existing deep snow cover, and fresh snowfall of 2 to 7 inches over 3 to 4 days, resulted in areas of blowing snow when moderately strong northwest winds developed near the eastern birder of South Dakota. While there were areas of low visibilities, the main problem appeared to be the drifting snow and icy roads which contributed to numerous accidents during and after the event. Reports from law enforcement indicated that the blowing snow coming across roads, combined with traffic, was putting a glaze on the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches over 3 days, and previously existing deep snow cover, resulted in drifting and areas of blowing snow when moderately strong northwest winds developed. Several accidents, including vehicles sliding into ditches, were reported during and after the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 1 to 3 inches, beginning early on February 13th, and existing deep snow cover, combined with strong northwest winds which developed on February 14th, to produce blowing and drifting snow which reduced visibilities in some areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Existing deep snow cover, and fresh snowfall of 2 to 7 inches over 3 to 4 days, resulted in areas of blowing snow when moderately strong northwest winds developed near the eastern birder of South Dakota. While there were areas of low visibilities, the main problem appeared to be the drifting snow and icy roads which contributed to numerous accidents during and after the event. Reports from law enforcement indicated that the blowing snow coming across roads, combined with traffic, was putting a glaze on the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 5 inches over 3 days, and previously existing deep snow cover, resulted in drifting and areas of blowing snow when moderately strong northwest winds developed. Several accidents, including vehicles sliding into ditches, were reported during and after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River, which began in March from snowmelt, continued through June with the river rising again due to heavy rain. Moderate to major flooding was observed, with the river at Mitchell rising to its highest crest for the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recurring heavy rainfall caused major flooding of Firesteel Creek from early June to just before the end of the month. The creek crested at 7.3 feet above flood stage near Mount Vernon on June 13th, or about three quarters of a foot below the record March crest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the James River was observed for most of the month. The river, which has been slowly falling before June, rose again due to heavy rain, and the number of fields and roads flooded increased again. The river crested at around 6.5 feet above flood stage near Scotland, which was just slightly below the March crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recurring heavy rain caused minor to marginally major flooding of the Big Sioux River beginning on June 13th. Flooding decreased late in June, ending along part of the river, but continuing past the end of the month in other places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recurring heavy rain cause minor to moderate flooding of the Big Sioux River. Some l roads and fields along the river were flooded. While the river remained a little below flood stage at Flandreau, flooding occured both north and south of that point, with the moderate flooding near the northern edge of the county. The flooding abated late in the month, but minor flooding developed again after June 27th as more heavy rain fell.

The river was at its highest levels during the first few days of the month, although it remained below its spring crest at Mitchell. The river then fell slowly. Flooding of fields and some roads slowly abated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a tornado, damaging winds, and flash flooding in southeast South Dakota, mostly near Interstate 90, during the evening of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado near the county line raised dust, but caused no reported damage. The tornado crossed briefly into Miner County before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a tornado, damaging winds, and flash flooding in southeast South Dakota, mostly near Interstate 90, during the evening of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused no reported damage. The tornado crossed into Miner County from Hanson County before quickly dissipating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a tornado, damaging winds, and flash flooding in southeast South Dakota, mostly near Interstate 90, during the evening of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding of several streets in the town of Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain along and near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of low lying yards and streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over southern Tripp County, then moved northeastward and dropped large hail in Colome.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the Vermillion River, the East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers, and the Little Vermillion River, caused by late July heavy rains, continued through early August, ending by the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Vermillion River, triggered by late July heavy rains, continued into early August before abating. Lowlands, farmland, and a few roads were flooded. The river crested at 3.55 feet above flood stage near Wakonda on August 4th, then fell below flood stage on August 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain along and near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of August 8th.
Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain along and near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of August 8th.
Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain along and near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of up to 3 inches in a short time caused flash flooding of streets, ditches, and other low areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second stronger surface low pressure area moved across the region on New Year's Eve bringing widespread heavy snowfall along with blizzard conditions. Bitter cold northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph combined with additional snowfall of 6 to 10 inches brought visibilities to near zero across much of the region. This was the second blizzard in two days across the region. The blizzard conditions continued into early New Year's Day.  Both Interstates 29 and 90 were closed from the 31st until Sunday, January 2nd. There were several stranded motorists along Highway 83 with five people being rescued.||The total snowfall amounts from the two storms ranged from 6 to 15 inches across the region.  The two day snowfall amounts included 6 inches at Eagle Butte; 7 inches at Doland; 8 inches at Mobridge and Gann Valley; 9 inches at Castlewood; 10 inches at Murdo, Clark, Ipswich, Kennebec, and Watertown and 11 inches at Clear Lake and Bryant.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included 12 inches at Aberdeen, Gettysburg, Highmore, Milbank, Mission Ridge, and Bowdle; 13 inches at Eureka, Pierre, Onida, and Blunt; 14 inches at Mellette, Sisseton, Victor, and Roscoe with 15 inches at Britton, Webster, and Redfield.  The snowfall began between 6 am and noon CST on the 31st and ended between 4 am and 11 am CST on the January 1st.

Snowfall ranging from 2 to 8 inches was accompanied by sustained northwest winds which reached 40 mph at times, with gusts as high as 55 mph. The snowfall, strong winds, and existing snow cover resulted in widespread blizzard conditions. Travel was made impossible in much of the area. There were several accidents and vehicles going into ditches, attributed to slick roads and low visibilities. Several motorists were stranded. Businesses were forced to close, and several school and other weekend activities were canceled or postponed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 3 inches, existing snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to around 50 mph combined to cause widespread blizzard conditions with frequent zero visibilities and dangerous wind chills. The storm made travel difficult to impossible, with several accidents reported, and some motorists becoming stranded. Numerous businesses were forced to close, and weekend activities including some school events were canceled or postponed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches and winds gusting to over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions on New Years Eve in southeast South Dakota near and west of a Pickstown to Mitchell to De Smet line. Roads were closed and many businesses were forced to close as travel became difficult to impossible. Lesser snow amounts with strong winds produced winter storm conditions east of the blizzard area, with very low visibilities not as widespread. The storm continued to midnight, or the end of the year 2010, in most areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches was accompanied by winds gusting to around 40 mph, producing widespread blowing snow with visibilities frequently below a half mile. Travel was made very difficult. Some businesses and roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure trough and associated surface low pressure area moved across the region bringing the first of two consecutive blizzards to central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches combined with bitter cold north winds of 25 to 40 mph caused widespread blizzard conditions across central and northeast South Dakota from the late morning until the evening hours. Near zero visibilities caused dangerous travel conditions resulting in the closing of Interstates 29 and 90 along with several highways across the region.  Several hundred people were stranded as a result of the storm. A group of fishermen had to be rescued in Day county when they became stranded on the ice.  The snowfall began across the area anywhere from 7 to 11 am CST and ended between 10 pm and 1 am CST.

This was the second blizzard in two days across the region. The blizzard conditions continued into early New Year's Day.  Both Interstates 29 and 90 were closed from the 31st until Sunday, January 2nd. There were several stranded motorists along Highway 83 with five people being rescued.||The total snowfall amounts from the two storms ranged from 6 to 15 inches across the region.  The two day snowfall amounts included 6 inches at Eagle Butte; 7 inches at Doland; 8 inches at Mobridge and Gann Valley; 9 inches at Castlewood; 10 inches at Murdo, Clark, Ipswich, Kennebec, and Watertown and 11 inches at Clear Lake and Bryant.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included 12 inches at Aberdeen, Gettysburg, Highmore, Milbank, Mission Ridge, and Bowdle; 13 inches at Eureka, Pierre, Onida, and Blunt; 14 inches at Mellette, Sisseton, Victor, and Roscoe with 15 inches at Britton, Webster, and Redfield.  The snowfall began between 6 am and noon CST on the 31st and ended between 4 am and 11 am CST on the January 1st.

A strong Alberta Clipper came across the region bringing snowfall, strong northwest winds, along with bitter cold Arctic air from the early morning until the late  afternoon.  Snowfall of 1 to 5 inches combined with 25 to 35 mph winds gusting to 45 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions across much of northeast South Dakota.  Travel was significantly disrupted or halted as a result with many events cancelled. The blizzard was short-lived from the mid morning hours into the early afternoon hours. The snowfall began between midnight and 2 am CST and ended from 2 to 4 pm CST in the afternoon.

Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches and winds gusting to over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions on New Years Eve in southeast South Dakota near and west of a Pickstown to Mitchell to De Smet line. Roads were closed and many businesses were forced to close as travel became difficult to impossible. Lesser snow amounts with strong winds produced winter storm conditions east of the blizzard area, with very low visibilities not as widespread. The storm continued to midnight, or the end of the year 2010, in most areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches and north to northwest winds gusting over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions with near zero visibilities in many areas. The 10 inch accumulation was reported at De Smet. Businesses and roads were closed by the storm with travel made difficult to impossible. The storm continued to midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure trough and associated surface low pressure area moved across the region bringing the first of two consecutive blizzards to central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches combined with bitter cold north winds of 25 to 40 mph caused widespread blizzard conditions across central and northeast South Dakota from the late morning until the evening hours. Near zero visibilities caused dangerous travel conditions resulting in the closing of Interstates 29 and 90 along with several highways across the region.  Several hundred people were stranded as a result of the storm. A group of fishermen had to be rescued in Day county when they became stranded on the ice.  The snowfall began across the area anywhere from 7 to 11 am CST and ended between 10 pm and 1 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced a light ice coating on driving and walking surfaces that caused several accidents and caused other vehicles to slide off roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused icy areas of roads and walking surfaces. Several accidents and other vehicles sliding off roads were reported. One of the accidents involved four vehicles on Interstate 90, injuring 4 people.

Snowfall ranging from 2 to 8 inches was accompanied by sustained northwest winds which reached 40 mph at times, with gusts as high as 55 mph. Businesses were forced to close, and several school and other weekend activities were canceled or postponed.

Numerous businesses were forced to close, and weekend activities including some school events were canceled or postponed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast across the region from Sunday night through the morning hours on Monday.  While this system brought snowfall amounts generally from 1 to 5 inches, a narrow frontal band of 6 to 12 inches occurred over north central South Dakota.  A fatality occurred in a two vehicle accident in Mcpherson county. Snowfall amounts in this southwest to northeast frontal band included; 6 inches in Eureka; 7 inches in Roscoe and 5W Sully Buttes; 8 inches in Gettysburg; 9 inches in Bowdle and 11E of Hosmer; 10 inches in Leola with a foot of snow falling 4 miles northwest of Elm Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 70 year old Hosmer man died on the 22nd in a two vehicle accident on South Dakota Highway 10 three miles west of Eureka. He was a passenger in a pickup truck that lost control on the heavy snowpacked highway and collided with a van. The driver of the pickup and the people in the van were also injured but, not seriously.

A strong low pressure system moved across the Central Plains, bringing snow to much of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills, where significant upslope enhanced snowfall developed. One to two feet of snow were common across the northern Black Hills, with six to 12 inches across much of the northern Foot Hills. As much as 40 inches of snow was reported in the Lead area. Gusty winds combined with the heavy snow to produce visibilities below a quarter mile.

Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches and winds gusting to over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions on New Years Eve in southeast South Dakota near and west of a Pickstown to Mitchell to De Smet line. Roads were closed and many businesses were forced to close as travel became difficult to impossible. Lesser snow amounts with strong winds produced winter storm conditions east of the blizzard area, with very low visibilities not as widespread. The storm continued to midnight, or the end of the year 2010, in most areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second stronger surface low pressure area moved across the region on New Year's Eve bringing widespread heavy snowfall along with blizzard conditions. Bitter cold northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph combined with additional snowfall of 6 to 10 inches brought visibilities to near zero across much of the region. This was the second blizzard in two days across the region. The blizzard conditions continued into early New Year's Day.  Both Interstates 29 and 90 were closed from the 31st until Sunday, January 2nd. There were several stranded motorists along Highway 83 with five people being rescued.||The total snowfall amounts from the two storms ranged from 6 to 15 inches across the region.  The two day snowfall amounts included 6 inches at Eagle Butte; 7 inches at Doland; 8 inches at Mobridge and Gann Valley; 9 inches at Castlewood; 10 inches at Murdo, Clark, Ipswich, Kennebec, and Watertown and 11 inches at Clear Lake and Bryant.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included 12 inches at Aberdeen, Gettysburg, Highmore, Milbank, Mission Ridge, and Bowdle; 13 inches at Eureka, Pierre, Onida, and Blunt; 14 inches at Mellette, Sisseton, Victor, and Roscoe with 15 inches at Britton, Webster, and Redfield.  The snowfall began between 6 am and noon CST on the 31st and ended between 4 am and 11 am CST on the January 1st.

A strong upper level low pressure trough and associated surface low pressure area moved across the region bringing the first of two consecutive blizzards to central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches combined with bitter cold north winds of 25 to 40 mph caused widespread blizzard conditions across central and northeast South Dakota from the late morning until the evening hours. Near zero visibilities caused dangerous travel conditions resulting in the closing of Interstates 29 and 90 along with several highways across the region.  Several hundred people were stranded as a result of the storm. A group of fishermen had to be rescued in Day county when they became stranded on the ice.  The snowfall began across the area anywhere from 7 to 11 am CST and ended between 10 pm and 1 am CST.

Snow accumulated 3 to 8 inches in southeast South Dakota north of Interstate 90 during the morning and early afternoon of December 20th. Accumulations of greater than 6 inches were reported in the Brookings, Madison, and Flandreau areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow during the morning and early afternoon accumulated 4 to 8 inches, with 7.7 inches reported near Flandreau.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow during the morning and early afternoon accumulated 2 to 5 inches, with 5 inches reported at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 8 inches in southeast South Dakota north of Interstate 90 during the morning and early afternoon of December 20th. Accumulations of greater than 6 inches were reported in the Brookings, Madison, and Flandreau areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow during the morning and early afternoon accumulated about 4 inches along the northern edge of McCook County, with accumulations dropping off to 2 inches further south. Snowfall of 3.5 inches was reported at Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent north to northwest winds combined with very cold air to produce wind chill values the frequently dropped to 35 below zero or a little colder. These extremely dangerous wind chills added to the hazards produced by the preceding blizzard. As winds slowly subsided during the night of January 7th, temperatures continued to drop to well below zero, keeping the wind chills at the very dangerous level through the morning of January 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Thursday, January 7th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread heavy snowfall over much of northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches. The snowfall began across northeast South Dakota in the late evening of the 5th into the early morning hours of the 6th. Many schools were closed on the 6th and the 7th. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches in Andover and Doland; 7 inches in Britton, Sisseton, and near Milbank; 8 inches in Aberdeen, Bryant, and near Summit; 9 inches at Wilmot and Castlewood; 10 inches in Clear Lake and 11 inches at Watertown.

A large upper level low pressure area combined with a cold surface high pressure area building in from the north brought light snow and very strong winds creating widespread blizzard conditions across north central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts of 1 to 4 inches combined with north winds of 25 to 40 mph gusting up to 55 mph created frequent whiteout conditions across the area.  Travel was significantly affected or halted and several schools were closed. Interstate-29 was closed from the North Dakota border and south on the 25th until the morning of the 26th.  The blizzard hampered efforts to restore power to the thousands of customers from the previous winter storm. The snowfall began in the morning hours from 6 to 10 am and ended around the time the blizzard conditions subsided.

Freezing rain produced ice accumulations around a quarter inch or a little more in Union County in extreme southeast South Dakota. Travel and walking were made extremely hazardous, and power lines and trees were damaged. Lesser ice accumulations were reported to the north and west, to the Sioux Falls and Yankton areas, producing slippery conditions in those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations of a quarter to a half inch. Travel and walking were made extremely hazardous and difficult. Damage to trees and power lines was reported, and there were scattered power outages. Schools were closed and several accidents were reported, including one accident involving 5 semis on Interstate 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Wednesday, January 6th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread snowfall over much of central and north central South Dakota. |Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 9 inches of fluffy snow. The snowfall began across central and north central South Dakota from the late afternoon through the late evening of the 5th. As the system tracked east, behind it strong north to northwest winds gusted to as high as 50 mph resulting in widespread blizzard conditions with near whiteout conditions at times.  In fact, Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties was closed for a time due to poor travel conditions. Schools throughout the region were also closed on the 6th, with some closed on the 7th.  While there were several accidents reported, none resulted in major injures. The snow along with the strong winds also collapsed a hay shed.  Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at Blunt and Timber Lake; 4 inches at Gettysburg and east of Hayes; 6 inches at Pierre, McLaughlin and Pollock; 7 inches at Gann Valley, Murdo, Eureka, Mobridge, Leola, Faulkton, and Ipswich, and 9 inches near McIntosh and Roscoe.

New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent north to northwest winds combined with very cold air to produce wind chill values the frequently dropped to 35 below zero or a little colder. These extremely dangerous wind chills added to the hazards produced by the preceding blizzard. As winds slowly subsided during the night of January 7th, temperatures continued to drop to well below zero, keeping the wind chills at the very dangerous level through the morning of January 8th.

A powerful mid season winter storm moved northeast out of the four corners region of the United States and into the Northern Plains.  Ahead of this system, warm and moist air streamed northward creating widespread fog and freezing fog conditions during the days leading up to the event.  Heavy riming frost began to accumulate on power lines and tower guide wires, placing heavy strain on them by the time the freezing rain arrived in the late morning and afternoon hours on Friday, January 22nd.  Along with the freezing rain, southeast winds gusting over 30 mph also created a strain on sagging power lines.  Scattered power outages were reported as early as Tuesday, January 19th due to the frost covered lines, but the majority of power line and power pole damage occurred during the evening of the 22nd and the morning of the 23rd.  The freezing rain that arrived during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd was the proverbial ���straw that broke the camel���s back���.  By the time the rain, freezing rain, and snow ended Saturday morning, January 23rd, nearly every power cooperative across central and northeast South Dakota suffered extensive power pole and power line damage. Also, several radio and television towers were downed by the icing and strong winds.||The heavy icing and strong winds downed over 5000 power poles along with 21,000 miles of power lines across South Dakota leaving thousands of households without power.  Several homes sustained substantial damage caused by broken water pipes. Power was still not restored for many customers until several weeks after the event.  Power line crews from Minnesota, Kansas, and Oklahoma were called upon to help restore power.  Several counties, along with the state emergency operations center, opened emergency shelters for people to stay.  Forty-one National Guard members were on active duty across the state helping to restore power.  Many flights were delayed or cancelled at several airports.  The ice and the wind also helped topple a canopy at a truck stop at the intersection of Highway 20 and 212.  On January 23rd, a radio and television signal transmission tower northwest of South Shore was downed along with a tower north of Reliance and a radio tower southwest of Marvin.  ||The hardest hit area with this storm was the Cheyenne River and Standing Rock Sioux reservations in central and north central South Dakota.  With no electricity, residents were dependent on donations of food, bottled water, blankets, heat and light sources, toiletries, and cots.  The rural water system serving the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe was shut down resulting in the state EOC shipping water to the reservation.||The Governor asked for a presidential disaster declaration for most of the counties and three reservations. The request was for both public and individual assistance for total damages estimated over 20 million dollars for the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Wessington weather radio tower had collapsed from frost and ice accumulation.

A powerful mid season winter storm moved northeast out of the four corners region of the United States and into the Northern Plains.  Ahead of this system, warm and moist air streamed northward creating widespread fog and freezing fog conditions during the days leading up to the event.  Heavy riming frost began to accumulate on power lines and tower guide wires, placing heavy strain on them by the time the freezing rain arrived in the late morning and afternoon hours on Friday, January 22nd.  Along with the freezing rain, southeast winds gusting over 30 mph also created a strain on sagging power lines.  Scattered power outages were reported as early as Tuesday, January 19th due to the frost covered lines, but the majority of power line and power pole damage occurred during the evening of the 22nd and the morning of the 23rd.  The freezing rain that arrived during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd was the proverbial ���straw that broke the camel���s back���.  By the time the rain, freezing rain, and snow ended Saturday morning, January 23rd, nearly every power cooperative across central and northeast South Dakota suffered extensive power pole and power line damage. Also, several radio and television towers were downed by the icing and strong winds.||The heavy icing and strong winds downed over 5000 power poles along with 21,000 miles of power lines across South Dakota leaving thousands of households without power.  Several homes sustained substantial damage caused by broken water pipes. Power was still not restored for many customers until several weeks after the event.  Power line crews from Minnesota, Kansas, and Oklahoma were called upon to help restore power.  Several counties, along with the state emergency operations center, opened emergency shelters for people to stay.  Forty-one National Guard members were on active duty across the state helping to restore power.  Many flights were delayed or cancelled at several airports.  The ice and the wind also helped topple a canopy at a truck stop at the intersection of Highway 20 and 212.  On January 23rd, a radio and television signal transmission tower northwest of South Shore was downed along with a tower north of Reliance and a radio tower southwest of Marvin.  ||The hardest hit area with this storm was the Cheyenne River and Standing Rock Sioux reservations in central and north central South Dakota.  With no electricity, residents were dependent on donations of food, bottled water, blankets, heat and light sources, toiletries, and cots.  The rural water system serving the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe was shut down resulting in the state EOC shipping water to the reservation.||The Governor asked for a presidential disaster declaration for most of the counties and three reservations. The request was for both public and individual assistance for total damages estimated over 20 million dollars for the state.

A large upper level low pressure area combined with a cold surface high pressure area building in from the north brought light snow and very strong winds creating widespread blizzard conditions across north central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts of 1 to 4 inches combined with north winds of 25 to 40 mph gusting up to 55 mph created frequent whiteout conditions across the area.  Travel was significantly affected or halted and several schools were closed. Interstate-29 was closed from the North Dakota border and south on the 25th until the morning of the 26th.  The blizzard hampered efforts to restore power to the thousands of customers from the previous winter storm. The snowfall began in the morning hours from 6 to 10 am and ended around the time the blizzard conditions subsided.

Arctic high pressure combined with strong northwest winds resulting in extreme wind chills from 35 to nearly 50 degrees below zero across central and northeast South Dakota.  Some of the lowest wind chills included, -40 in Aberdeen; -41 in Watertown; -42 in Highmore; -43 in Leola and Faulkton; -44 in Eagle Butte, Herreid, and Gettysburg; and -47 in Bowdle. Several record lows were also tied or broken during the morning hours of the 8th including, -22 degrees NW of Gann Valley and Victor; -23 degrees at Pierre and Sisseton; -24 degrees at Roscoe; and -34 degrees at Pollock.

New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent north to northwest winds combined with very cold air to produce wind chill values the frequently dropped to 35 below zero or a little colder. These extremely dangerous wind chills added to the hazards produced by the preceding winter storm. As winds slowly subsided during the night of January 7th, temperatures continued to drop to well below zero, keeping the wind chills at the very dangerous level through the morning of January 8th.

A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Wednesday, January 6th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread snowfall over much of central and north central South Dakota. |Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 9 inches of fluffy snow. The snowfall began across central and north central South Dakota from the late afternoon through the late evening of the 5th. As the system tracked east, behind it strong north to northwest winds gusted to as high as 50 mph resulting in widespread blizzard conditions with near whiteout conditions at times.  In fact, Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties was closed for a time due to poor travel conditions. Schools throughout the region were also closed on the 6th, with some closed on the 7th.  While there were several accidents reported, none resulted in major injures. The snow along with the strong winds also collapsed a hay shed.  Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at Blunt and Timber Lake; 4 inches at Gettysburg and east of Hayes; 6 inches at Pierre, McLaughlin and Pollock; 7 inches at Gann Valley, Murdo, Eureka, Mobridge, Leola, Faulkton, and Ipswich, and 9 inches near McIntosh and Roscoe.

A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the region. The first part of the storm produced heavy snow on Dec 30. The main system moved across the Central Plains with additional snow and strong northwest winds on the plains on Dec 31. Snowfall ranged from six to 12 inches, with higher amounts across the northern Black Hills and portions of southwest and south central South Dakota. Northwest winds gusting to 50 mph produced blizzard conditions with visibilities near zero at times across much of the western and south central South Dakota plains on Dec 31. Interstate 90 was closed for nearly 24 hours from Rapid City across South Dakota to Sioux Falls. With temperatures near zero, wind chill readings were 20 to 35 below zero.

Northwest winds gusting to 50 mph produced blizzard conditions with visibilities near zero at times across much of the western and south central South Dakota plains on Dec 31. Interstate 90 was closed for nearly 24 hours from Rapid City across South Dakota to Sioux Falls. With temperatures near zero, wind chill readings were 20 to 35 below zero.

A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Wednesday, January 6th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread snowfall over much of central and north central South Dakota. |Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 9 inches of fluffy snow. The snowfall began across central and north central South Dakota from the late afternoon through the late evening of the 5th. As the system tracked east, behind it strong north to northwest winds gusted to as high as 50 mph resulting in widespread blizzard conditions with near whiteout conditions at times.  In fact, Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties was closed for a time due to poor travel conditions. Schools throughout the region were also closed on the 6th, with some closed on the 7th.  While there were several accidents reported, none resulted in major injures. The snow along with the strong winds also collapsed a hay shed.  Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at Blunt and Timber Lake; 4 inches at Gettysburg and east of Hayes; 6 inches at Pierre, McLaughlin and Pollock; 7 inches at Gann Valley, Murdo, Eureka, Mobridge, Leola, Faulkton, and Ipswich, and 9 inches near McIntosh and Roscoe.

A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Thursday, January 7th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread heavy snowfall over much of northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches. The snowfall began across northeast South Dakota in the late evening of the 5th into the early morning hours of the 6th. Many schools were closed on the 6th and the 7th. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches in Andover and Doland; 7 inches in Britton, Sisseton, and near Milbank; 8 inches in Aberdeen, Bryant, and near Summit; 9 inches at Wilmot and Castlewood; 10 inches in Clear Lake and 11 inches at Watertown.

Arctic high pressure combined with strong northwest winds resulting in extreme wind chills from 35 to nearly 50 degrees below zero across central and northeast South Dakota.  Some of the lowest wind chills included, -40 in Aberdeen; -41 in Watertown; -42 in Highmore; -43 in Leola and Faulkton; -44 in Eagle Butte, Herreid, and Gettysburg; and -47 in Bowdle. Several record lows were also tied or broken during the morning hours of the 8th including, -22 degrees NW of Gann Valley and Victor; -23 degrees at Pierre and Sisseton; -24 degrees at Roscoe; and -34 degrees at Pollock.

Freezing rain produced ice accumulations around a quarter inch or a little more in Union County in extreme southeast South Dakota. Travel and walking were made extremely hazardous, and power lines and trees were damaged. Lesser ice accumulations were reported to the north and west, to the Sioux Falls and Yankton areas, producing slippery conditions in those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle, mixed at times with snow, produced a light glazing of ice, making driving and walking surfaces very slick. A man from Minnesota was killed when his vehicle slid off Interstate 90 near Brandon and rolled in a ditch.

A large upper level low pressure area combined with a cold surface high pressure area building in from the north brought light snow and very strong winds creating widespread blizzard conditions across north central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts of 1 to 4 inches combined with north winds of 25 to 40 mph gusting up to 55 mph created frequent whiteout conditions across the area.  Travel was significantly affected or halted and several schools were closed. Interstate-29 was closed from the North Dakota border and south on the 25th until the morning of the 26th.  The blizzard hampered efforts to restore power to the thousands of customers from the previous winter storm. The snowfall began in the morning hours from 6 to 10 am and ended around the time the blizzard conditions subsided.

Travel was significantly affected or halted and several schools were closed. Interstate-29 was closed from the North Dakota border and south on the 25th until the morning of the 26th.  The blizzard hampered efforts to restore power to the thousands of customers from the previous winter storm. The snowfall began in the morning hours from 6 to 10 am and ended around the time the blizzard conditions subsided.

New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent north to northwest winds combined with very cold air to produce wind chill values the frequently dropped to 35 below zero or a little colder. These extremely dangerous wind chills added to the hazards produced by the preceding winter storm. As winds slowly subsided during the night of January 7th, temperatures continued to drop to well below zero, keeping the wind chills at the very dangerous level through the morning of January 8th.

Dense freezing fog created very low visibilities to 1/16 of a mile along with icy roads which contributed to a multi-vehicle wreck 11 miles south of Watertown. The accident killed three people and injured two others. Five vehicles, including a grain truck, were involved in the wreck about 930 am near the intersection of U.S. Highway 81 and State Highway 22.

A powerful mid season winter storm moved northeast out of the four corners region of the United States and into the Northern Plains.  Ahead of this system, warm and moist air streamed northward creating widespread fog and freezing fog conditions during the days leading up to the event.  Heavy riming frost began to accumulate on power lines and tower guide wires, placing heavy strain on them by the time the freezing rain arrived in the late morning and afternoon hours on Friday, January 22nd.  Along with the freezing rain, southeast winds gusting over 30 mph also created a strain on sagging power lines.  Scattered power outages were reported as early as Tuesday, January 19th due to the frost covered lines, but the majority of power line and power pole damage occurred during the evening of the 22nd and the morning of the 23rd.  The freezing rain that arrived during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd was the proverbial ���straw that broke the camel���s back���.  By the time the rain, freezing rain, and snow ended Saturday morning, January 23rd, nearly every power cooperative across central and northeast South Dakota suffered extensive power pole and power line damage. Also, several radio and television towers were downed by the icing and strong winds.||The heavy icing and strong winds downed over 5000 power poles along with 21,000 miles of power lines across South Dakota leaving thousands of households without power.  Several homes sustained substantial damage caused by broken water pipes. Power was still not restored for many customers until several weeks after the event.  Power line crews from Minnesota, Kansas, and Oklahoma were called upon to help restore power.  Several counties, along with the state emergency operations center, opened emergency shelters for people to stay.  Forty-one National Guard members were on active duty across the state helping to restore power.  Many flights were delayed or cancelled at several airports.  The ice and the wind also helped topple a canopy at a truck stop at the intersection of Highway 20 and 212.  On January 23rd, a radio and television signal transmission tower northwest of South Shore was downed along with a tower north of Reliance and a radio tower southwest of Marvin.  ||The hardest hit area with this storm was the Cheyenne River and Standing Rock Sioux reservations in central and north central South Dakota.  With no electricity, residents were dependent on donations of food, bottled water, blankets, heat and light sources, toiletries, and cots.  The rural water system serving the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe was shut down resulting in the state EOC shipping water to the reservation.||The Governor asked for a presidential disaster declaration for most of the counties and three reservations. The request was for both public and individual assistance for total damages estimated over 20 million dollars for the state.

A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Wednesday, January 6th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread snowfall over much of central and north central South Dakota. |Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 9 inches of fluffy snow. The snowfall began across central and north central South Dakota from the late afternoon through the late evening of the 5th. As the system tracked east, behind it strong north to northwest winds gusted to as high as 50 mph resulting in widespread blizzard conditions with near whiteout conditions at times.  In fact, Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties was closed for a time due to poor travel conditions. Schools throughout the region were also closed on the 6th, with some closed on the 7th.  While there were several accidents reported, none resulted in major injures. The snow along with the strong winds also collapsed a hay shed.  Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at Blunt and Timber Lake; 4 inches at Gettysburg and east of Hayes; 6 inches at Pierre, McLaughlin and Pollock; 7 inches at Gann Valley, Murdo, Eureka, Mobridge, Leola, Faulkton, and Ipswich, and 9 inches near McIntosh and Roscoe.

Arctic high pressure combined with strong northwest winds resulting in extreme wind chills from 35 to nearly 50 degrees below zero across central and northeast South Dakota.  Some of the lowest wind chills included, -40 in Aberdeen; -41 in Watertown; -42 in Highmore; -43 in Leola and Faulkton; -44 in Eagle Butte, Herreid, and Gettysburg; and -47 in Bowdle. Several record lows were also tied or broken during the morning hours of the 8th including, -22 degrees NW of Gann Valley and Victor; -23 degrees at Pierre and Sisseton; -24 degrees at Roscoe; and -34 degrees at Pollock.

New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent north to northwest winds combined with very cold air to produce wind chill values the frequently dropped to 35 below zero or a little colder. These extremely dangerous wind chills added to the hazards produced by the preceding winter storm. As winds slowly subsided during the night of January 7th, temperatures continued to drop to well below zero, keeping the wind chills at the very dangerous level through the morning of January 8th.

A large upper level low pressure area combined with a cold surface high pressure area building in from the north brought light snow and very strong winds creating widespread blizzard conditions across north central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts of 1 to 4 inches combined with north winds of 25 to 40 mph gusting up to 55 mph created frequent whiteout conditions across the area.  Travel was significantly affected or halted and several schools were closed. Interstate-29 was closed from the North Dakota border and south on the 25th until the morning of the 26th.  The blizzard hampered efforts to restore power to the thousands of customers from the previous winter storm. The snowfall began in the morning hours from 6 to 10 am and ended around the time the blizzard conditions subsided.

A powerful mid season winter storm moved northeast out of the four corners region of the United States and into the Northern Plains.  Ahead of this system, warm and moist air streamed northward creating widespread fog and freezing fog conditions during the days leading up to the event.  Heavy riming frost began to accumulate on power lines and tower guide wires, placing heavy strain on them by the time the freezing rain arrived in the late morning and afternoon hours on Friday, January 22nd.  Along with the freezing rain, southeast winds gusting over 30 mph also created a strain on sagging power lines.  Scattered power outages were reported as early as Tuesday, January 19th due to the frost covered lines, but the majority of power line and power pole damage occurred during the evening of the 22nd and the morning of the 23rd.  The freezing rain that arrived during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd was the proverbial ���straw that broke the camel���s back���.  By the time the rain, freezing rain, and snow ended Saturday morning, January 23rd, nearly every power cooperative across central and northeast South Dakota suffered extensive power pole and power line damage. Also, several radio and television towers were downed by the icing and strong winds.||The heavy icing and strong winds downed over 5000 power poles along with 21,000 miles of power lines across South Dakota leaving thousands of households without power.  Several homes sustained substantial damage caused by broken water pipes. Power was still not restored for many customers until several weeks after the event.  Power line crews from Minnesota, Kansas, and Oklahoma were called upon to help restore power.  Several counties, along with the state emergency operations center, opened emergency shelters for people to stay.  Forty-one National Guard members were on active duty across the state helping to restore power.  Many flights were delayed or cancelled at several airports.  The ice and the wind also helped topple a canopy at a truck stop at the intersection of Highway 20 and 212.  On January 23rd, a radio and television signal transmission tower northwest of South Shore was downed along with a tower north of Reliance and a radio tower southwest of Marvin.  ||The hardest hit area with this storm was the Cheyenne River and Standing Rock Sioux reservations in central and north central South Dakota.  With no electricity, residents were dependent on donations of food, bottled water, blankets, heat and light sources, toiletries, and cots.  The rural water system serving the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe was shut down resulting in the state EOC shipping water to the reservation.||The Governor asked for a presidential disaster declaration for most of the counties and three reservations. The request was for both public and individual assistance for total damages estimated over 20 million dollars for the state.

Travel was significantly affected or halted and several schools were closed. Interstate-29 was closed from the North Dakota border and south on the 25th until the morning of the 26th.  The blizzard hampered efforts to restore power to the thousands of customers from the previous winter storm. The snowfall began in the morning hours from 6 to 10 am and ended around the time the blizzard conditions subsided.

A strong Alberta Clipper low pressure system tracked southeast through the northern plains on Tuesday night, January 5th through Wednesday, January 6th. Sufficient Pacific moisture interacted with bitter cold Arctic air surging south from Canada resulting in widespread snowfall over much of central and north central South Dakota. |Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 9 inches of fluffy snow. The snowfall began across central and north central South Dakota from the late afternoon through the late evening of the 5th. As the system tracked east, behind it strong north to northwest winds gusted to as high as 50 mph resulting in widespread blizzard conditions with near whiteout conditions at times.  In fact, Interstate 90 across Jones and Lyman counties was closed for a time due to poor travel conditions. Schools throughout the region were also closed on the 6th, with some closed on the 7th.  While there were several accidents reported, none resulted in major injures. The snow along with the strong winds also collapsed a hay shed.  Some snowfall amounts included, 2 inches at Blunt and Timber Lake; 4 inches at Gettysburg and east of Hayes; 6 inches at Pierre, McLaughlin and Pollock; 7 inches at Gann Valley, Murdo, Eureka, Mobridge, Leola, Faulkton, and Ipswich, and 9 inches near McIntosh and Roscoe.

An area of low pressure moved northeast across the region from Sunday night through the morning hours on Monday.  While this system brought snowfall amounts generally from 1 to 5 inches, a narrow frontal band of 6 to 12 inches occurred over north central South Dakota.  A fatality occurred in a two vehicle accident in Mcpherson county. Snowfall amounts in this southwest to northeast frontal band included; 6 inches in Eureka; 7 inches in Roscoe and 5W Sully Buttes; 8 inches in Gettysburg; 9 inches in Bowdle and 11E of Hosmer; 10 inches in Leola with a foot of snow falling 4 miles northwest of Elm Lake.

Light freezing drizzle caused many slippery roads across the area. The icy roads contributed to a two vehicle accident in Edmunds county which resulted in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15 year old boy lost control of his vehicle on an icy road and collided with another vehicle on South Dakota Highway 45 two miles south of Ipswich.  The teenager died at the scene. The driver of the other vehicle received minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 1 to 4 inches combined with deep existing snow  cover and strong northwest winds to produce blowing and drifting snow. The snow began on Saturday February 13th, while the strong winds and blowing snow were observed on Sunday February 14th into the morning of February 15th. The blowing and drifting snow and resulting accidents closed parts of Interstates 90 and 29 for a period, and several accidents were reported on other roads as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 1 to 2 inches, beginning early on February 13th, and existing deep snow cover, combined with strong northwest winds which developed on February 14th, to produce blowing and drifting snow which reduced visibilities in some areas. The developing slick roads resulted in several accidents, which continued after the snow had ended and visibilities had improved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 2 to 3 inches, beginning early on February 13th, and existing deep snow cover, combined with strong northwest winds which developed on February 14th, to produce blowing and drifting snow which reduced visibilities in some areas. The developing slick roads resulted in numerous accidents, which continued after the snow had ended and visibilities had improved. Most of the reported accidents were on Interstate 90. A sheriff's deputy investigating an accident had his vehicle, with him inside, slammed into at 45 mph, but no one was injured in the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 6 inches, previously existing snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to around 40 mph produced widespread blowing snow. Near blizzard conditions were produced in some areas, with visibilities locally a quarter mile or less. New snowfall included 6 inches at Sioux Falls. Schools and businesses were closed, and travel became nearly impossible in much of the area. The wind combined with cold temperatures to produce wind chills colder than 35 below zero during the latter part of the storm. This extreme cold continued into the next day, Friday, January 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph, along with existing loose snow cover, caused blowing snow with visibilities of a quarter mile or less in areas. The low visibilities were widespread in the Huron to De Smet area, where travel was impossible for most of the daytime hours. Elsewhere in southeast South Dakota, the worst conditions were more localized. Several schools, businesses, and roads, including parts of Interstates 90 and 29, closed as the blizzard developed. Travel remained difficult as the storm abated because of the heavy drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 45 mph combined with existing snow cover to cause blowing snow. there were a few areas where visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less. Some businesses, schools, and roads were closed because of the low visibilities and the drifting snow.

A compact but strong area of low pressure moved from Canada and across the Northern Plains from the 7th through the 9th. A prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow developed over far northeast South Dakota bringing 6 to 8 inches of snow to the area.  Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Milbank; 7 inches at Clear Lake and Sisseton; 8 inches at Summit, Victor, and Wilmot.

Existing deep snow cover, and fresh snowfall of 2 to 7 inches over 3 to 4 days, resulted in areas of blowing snow when moderately strong northwest winds developed near the eastern birder of South Dakota. While there were areas of low visibilities, the main problem appeared to be the drifting snow and icy roads which contributed to numerous accidents during and after the event. Reports from law enforcement indicated that the blowing snow coming across roads, combined with traffic, was putting a glaze on the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches over 3 days, and previously existing deep snow cover, resulted in drifting and areas of blowing snow when moderately strong northwest winds developed. Several accidents, including vehicles sliding into ditches, were reported during and after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice break-up along the White River caused from the Badlands to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River as flood waters reached the shoulder of South Dakota Highway 44. The river gage south of Interior along Highway 44 crested at 15.44 feet or 1.44 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff caused the Turtle Creek at Redfield to go above the flood stage of 7 feet on March 15th. The creek crested at 15.50 feet on March 20th and continued to flood throughout March. Some homes in Bula Park and Vinegar Flats were threatened and the bridge into Bula Park was flooded. Also some roads were flooded along the creek.
Snowmelt runoff caused the Oak Creek near Wakpala to go slightly over the flood stage of 10 feet to 10.21 feet at the end of March.

Snowmelt runoff from an expansive snow cover flooded many creeks, roads, along with thousands of acres of pasture and cropland throughout northeast South Dakota. There were numerous road closures. The flooding lasted through the end of the month and for many locations into April. The counties mainly affected were Brown, Marshall, Day, Spink, and Roberts. Numerous communities were affected including Aberdeen, Claremont, Waubay, Amherst, Kidder, and the Richmond Lake area. The Claremont, Amherst, and Britton areas were the hardest hit with flooded land and roads. Several farms were surrounded by water with some people stranded. Between Aberdeen and Britton, sixty percent of the land was under water. Thousands of acres of cropland will not be planted due to too much water with estimates that 20 to 25 percent of Brown county cropland would not be planted. Many people in northeast South Dakota have had too much water for many years. The road damage was extensive and repairs will be in the millions of dollars. Many roads across the area will also have to be raised.  Many people had extra long commutes due to flooded roads with some people having to move out of their homes.  Across Day and Marshall counties, rising lakes threatened many homes and cabins with sandbagging taking place. Most lakes and rivers across northeast South Dakota were at or near record levels.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were flooded throughout Spink county and damaged. Several bridges were also damaged by the flooding.  Snake Creek surrounded some homes between Redfield and Ashton and washed out part of Highway 212 near Frankfort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff caused Big Stone Lake in eastern Roberts county to rise several feet. The lake flooded several homes, businesses, buildings, recreation areas, and roads. Big Stone Lake rose to 973.55 feet above sea level. Extensive sandbagging took place along the lake with many structures and roads sustaining damage.
Ice break-up and several ice jams caused flooding along the White River across all of Jones and Lyman counties to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jamming caused minor flooding along the White River across Jones county. The river gage on the Highway 83 bridge south of Murdo crested at 14.9 feet, nearly a foot above flood stage. The river widened to nearly a half mile along Highway 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff brought the Maple River in north central Brown county above flood stage. The Maple River went above the flood stage of 10 feet on March 18th rising to a little over 15 feet before falling back to below flood stage on March 25th. Lowland flooding along with a few flooded roads occurred.

The James River flooded throughout Brown county beginning in the middle of March through the end of March and into April. The James River rose to around 6 feet above flood stage from Columbia to Stratford. The James River at Stratford reached a record crest of 20.08 feet on March 29th. Agricultural land and many roads were flooded along with several outbuildings flooded or threatened.

The James River flooded throughout Spink County beginning in the middle of March continuing through the end of March and into April. The James River rose up to 12 feet above flood stage at the gaging sites throughout the county. The James River at Ashton rose to 25.24 feet where the flood stage is 13 feet. The James River northeast of Redfield rose to 29.34 feet where the flood stage is 20 feet. The river flooded thousands of acres of crop and pastureland along with many roads. Also, many outbuildings were flooded along with several homes threatened.

Melting of a heavy snow cover caused major to record flooding of the Vermillion River beginning on March 12th, and continuing into late March. The flooding also affected the east and west Fork Vermillion Rivers, and the Little Vermillion River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack resulted in moderate to major flooding of the Vermillion River. Numerous fields and roads were flooded with some roads washed out. The Vermillion River crested at 3.6 feet above flood stage near Wakonda on March 14th, and 6.4 feet above flood stage near Vermillion on March 20th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack resulted in moderate to major flooding of the Little Vermillion River. Numerous fields and roads were flooded with some roads washed out. The Little Vermillion River crested at 2.3 feet above flood stage near Salem on March 14th.

Melting of a heavy snow cover caused major flooding of the Big Sioux River beginning on March 12th. Flooding decreased late in the month, ending along part of the river, but continuing past the end of March in other places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack caused flooding of Split Rock creek in the Corson area, with a crest of 2.5 feet above flood stage reported. The flooding affected  farmland near the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system brought a prolonged period of snow to northwestern South Dakota. Snowfall totals over a two day period ranged from four to eight inches, with locally higher amounts over the northern Black Hills and far northwestern South Dakota.
Ice break-up and several ice jams caused flooding along the White River across all of Jones and Lyman counties to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near record flooding occurred along the White River due to ice jamming. The river gage southwest of Oacoma along Highway 47 crested at 24.4 feet or 9.4 feet above flood stage. One home was threatened by the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Little Minnesota River near Peever flooded for nearly two weeks from mid to late March. The river went above the 17 foot flood stage on March 14th quickly rising to a record crest of 22.82 feet on March 15th. Mainly pasture and cropland flooding occurred along with a few roads being overtopped.

Minor flooding of the Big Sioux River occurred through Codington county. The river went above flood stage at all three gaging locations and continued above flood stage into April at the gage located three miles northwest of Watertown. The river rose from a tenth to almost 2 feet above flood stage at the gaging sites. Agricultural land along with few roads were flooded.

The Big Sioux River went above flood stage in the middle of March and remained above flood stage for one week. The river rose above flood stage of 11 feet to 12.29 feet at the gaging site at Castlewood. Minor flooding of agricultural land along with a few roads occurred.

Snowmelt runoff caused Big Stone Lake in northeast Grant county to rise several feet. The lake flooded several homes, businesses, buildings, recreation areas, and roads. Big Stone Lake rose to 973.55 feet above sea level. Extensive sandbagging took place along the lake. The hardest hit areas were homes and structures on the peninsula north of Big Stone City where a lot of damage occurred. Huge chunks of ice from the lake and the Whetstone River tore through the Big Stone City park and flooded Highway 12 for a short time.
Ice break-up along the White River caused from the Badlands to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River. The river gage on the U.S. Highway 83 bridge north of White River crested at 14.9 feet or 0.9 feet above flood stage. The river was reported to be a half mile wide near Highway 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice break-up along the White River caused from the Badlands to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near record flooding occurred along the White River. The river gage southwest of Oacoma along highway 47 crested at 24.4 feet or 9.4 feet above flood stage. One home was threatened due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system brought heavy snow to portions of Shannon and Bennett Counties in southwestern South Dakota. Rainfall changed to snow during the early morning of the 9th, with the heavy snow continuing through the early morning of the 10th. Snowfall amounts were generally in the four to eight inch range, with some higher elevations from Porcupine to Martin receiving around a foot of snow.

Runoff from snowmelt along with some ice jamming brought the Elm River above flood stage at all three gaging locations in Brown county. The river rose from 3 to almost 6 feet above flood stage.  At Westport, the Elm River rose above the flood stage of 14 feet to 19.78 feet. The Elm River nine miles west of Frederick reached a record crest of 15.96 feet on March 17th with a record crest of 15.69 feet near Ordway on March 18th. Significant lowland and cropland were flooded along with several roads. Also, several homes were threatened with some sandbagging taking place. Some outbuildings were flooded.

Snowmelt runoff from an expansive snow cover flooded many creeks, roads, along with thousands of acres of pasture and cropland throughout northeast South Dakota. There were numerous road closures. The flooding lasted through the end of the month and for many locations into April. The counties mainly affected were Brown, Marshall, Day, Spink, and Roberts. Numerous communities were affected including Aberdeen, Claremont, Waubay, Amherst, Kidder, and the Richmond Lake area. The Claremont, Amherst, and Britton areas were the hardest hit with flooded land and roads. Several farms were surrounded by water with some people stranded. Between Aberdeen and Britton, sixty percent of the land was under water. Thousands of acres of cropland will not be planted due to too much water with estimates that 20 to 25 percent of Brown county cropland would not be planted. Many people in northeast South Dakota have had too much water for many years. The road damage was extensive and repairs will be in the millions of dollars. Many roads across the area will also have to be raised.  Many people had extra long commutes due to flooded roads with some people having to move out of their homes.  Across Day and Marshall counties, rising lakes threatened many homes and cabins with sandbagging taking place. Most lakes and rivers across northeast South Dakota were at or near record levels.

Scattered light rain showers falling into a very dry airmass were responsible for several heat bursts that occurred across central South Dakota from Pierre to Onida during the evening hours of March 30th. Between 853 pm and 1053 pm CDT, observations from the Pierre airport (KPIR) showed a marked increase in temperature (+10F), decrease in dewpoint temperature (-4F), pressure falls, and gusty surface winds (a peak wind gust of 48 mph). On a farm outside of Pierre, winds were estimated up to 70 mph as some shingles were blown off the roof along with damage to several outbuildings. The Onida airport recorded a peak wind gust of 66 mph in the early evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack caused flooding of the Big Sioux River which resulted in moderate to major flooding. Several fields and roads were flooded. The river crested at 6.4 feet above flood stage several miles northeast of Richland on March 17th. A damaged dike near this location caused more widespread flooding of cropland, and also resulted in the flooding and closing of South Dakota Highway 48 in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack caused flooding of the Big Sioux River which resulted in minor to moderate flooding. Several fields and a few roads were flooded, as was the Flandreau city park. The river crested at 1.2 feet above flood stage at Flandreau on March 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding along with ice jamming on Medicine creek intruded on the town of Blunt. The creek overflowed and flooded parts of the east side of Blunt. One home was sandbagged with a mile and a half of road submerged by the two branches of the creek.
