EVENT_NARRATIVE: Foot Creek 6 miles northwest of Aberdeen reached a record stage of 10.38 feet on March 17th where flood stage is 9 feet.  The Moccasin creek also flooded in Aberdeen reaching 7.1 feet in north Aberdeen where flood stage is 6 feet. Eighty percent of the township roads in Brown county were closed. Volunteers, inmates, and the National Guard all took part in extensive sandbagging in the Camino Real subdivsion in Aberdeen, in Claremont, and Tacoma Park areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy snow cover caused major flooding of the James River beginning on March 11th, and continuing past the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snowpack caused flooding of the James River which became major during the middle and late part of the month. Numerous fields and roads were flooded, including South Dakota Highway 38 east of Mitchell. The river crested at 8.00 feet above flood stage near Mitchell on March 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy snow cover caused major to record flooding of the Vermillion River beginning on March 12th, and continuing into late March. The flooding also affected the east and west Fork Vermillion Rivers, and the Little Vermillion River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid melting of a heavy winter snowpack resulted in major to record flooding of the Vermillion River, including the east and West Fork Vermillion Rivers. Fields and most county and smaller roads were flooded with some roads washed out. U.S. Highway 18 near Davis was flooded and closed. Homes and businesses were flooded, especially in the hard hit town of Davis, but also in and near Centerville, where ice jams and at least two broken dikes added to the flooding problems. The Vermillion River crested at 5.4 feet above flood stage near Davis on March 15th. The East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers crested at 2 and 3.2 feet above flood stage, respectively, near Parker on march 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail at the town of Hitchcock.
Melting of a heavy snow cover caused major flooding of the James River beginning on March 11th, and continuing past the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snowpack caused record flooding of Firesteel Creek from March 12th through March 22nd. Firesteel Creek crested at 8 feet above flood stage near Mount Vernon on March 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River which began in March due to snowmelt continued through all of April, with the river falling slowly during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River continued from March through all of April. The river fell slowly during the month, with no new flooding, but farmland and some roads remained flooded. The river fell from 7.6 feet above flood stage at Huron at the start of April to 4 feet above flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed over the region during the night and early morning, bringing milder air over cold air at the surface. Light freezing rain developed over western South Dakota, mixing with snow and sleet at times. The heaviest freezing rain fell across southwestern South Dakota, including the Black Hills, where as much as a quarter inch of ice accumulated. Roads became ice covered and caused many accidents during the morning.

New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 7 inches, previously existing snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions, with visibilities less than a quarter mile. New snowfall included 7 inches at Wessington Springs. Schools and businesses were closed, and travel became impossible in much of the area. The wind combined with cold temperatures to produce wind chills colder than 35 below zero during the latter part of the storm. This extreme cold continued into the next day, Friday, January 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph, along with existing loose snow cover, caused blowing snow with visibilities of a quarter mile or less in areas. The low visibilities were widespread in the Huron to De Smet area, where travel was impossible for most of the daytime hours. Elsewhere in southeast South Dakota, the worst conditions were more localized. Several schools, businesses, and roads, including parts of Interstates 90 and 29, closed as the blizzard developed. Travel remained difficult as the storm abated because of the heavy drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 45 mph combined with existing snow cover to cause blowing snow. there were a few areas where visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less. Some businesses, schools, and roads were closed because of the low visibilities and the drifting snow.

Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph, along with existing loose snow cover, caused blowing snow with visibilities of a quarter mile or less in areas. The low visibilities were widespread in the Huron to De Smet area, where travel was impossible for most of the daytime hours. Elsewhere in southeast South Dakota, the worst conditions were more localized. Several schools, businesses, and roads, including parts of Interstates 90 and 29, closed as the blizzard developed. Travel remained difficult as the storm abated because of the heavy drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 45 mph combined with existing snow cover to cause blowing snow. there were a few areas where visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less. Some businesses, schools, and roads were closed because of the low visibilities and the drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: New snowfall varying from 2 to 8 inches, previous snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph combined to produce widespread blowing and drifting snow over all of southeast South Dakota. The worst conditions, with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile, were from the James to Missouri River valleys, or along and southwest of a line from Huron to Vermillion. These conditions were also observed more locally to the east of this area. Travel was brought to a standstill during the worst conditions. Even where visibilities were a little higher, travel was extremely difficult and dangerous because of the snowfall, drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills that accompanied the storm. The storm forced the closing of schools and businesses, and postponement of athletic events and other activities. The snow, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions took place on January 6th and 7th, while dangerous wind chills continued well into January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches, previously existing snow cover, and northwest winds gusting to around 40 mph produced widespread blowing snow. Near blizzard conditions were produced in some areas, with visibilities locally a quarter mile or less. New snowfall included 7.2 inches at Canton. Schools and businesses were closed, and travel became nearly impossible in much of the area. The wind combined with cold temperatures to produce wind chills colder than 35 below zero during the latter part of the storm. This extreme cold continued into the next day, Friday, January 8th.

A strong upper level wave combined with abundant moisture to produce strong thunderstorms over western South Dakota. Torrential rain of two to four inches fell across the eastern and southern slopes of the Black Hills in a brief period, causing flooding from Piedmont southward to the Nebraska border. Flood waters continued downstream on the Cheyenne River to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along the eastern Custer County foothills. Flooding was reported on Iron Creek, Battle Creek, Grace Coolidge Creek, Lame Johnny Creek, French Creek, and Beaver Creek. Several County roads were damaged from the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along the Fall River, Horsehead Creek, Hat Creek, Beaver Creek, and other smaller creeks and streams in Fall River County. The low water crossings in Hot Springs at South Sixth Street and at Joplin Avenue were flooded as well as several county roads. The creeks and streams were so high that Angostura Reservoir filled up and over 5900 cfs was being released. This was the first time in 10 years that Angostura Reservoir was full. The saturated soil and gusty winds also helped to bring down several large trees in Hot Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system and cold front produced strong winds across southwestern South Dakota. Ahead of the low, strong south to southwest winds developed across south central South Dakota during the early afternoon. A severe thunderstorm produced strong wind gusts across eastern Todd and Mellette Counties.

Thunderstorms produced damaging winds over a long path as they moved from west to east over southeast South Dakota during the evening of May 24th. The storms were mostly south of Interstate 90, although there were a few storms north of the Interstate early in the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed a garage and damaged three vehicles in the garage. The winds blew the roof off the garage and partially collapsed the three walls that were not part of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow set up across Corson and Dewey counties during the early morning hours of April 2nd. Along with with heavy wet snow, northwest winds gusting up to 40 mph developed. By the time the snow ended in the late morning hours, 6 to 8 inches of snow had fallen. The heavy snow, combined with the strong winds, downed many power poles across the region along with making travel treacherous. Some snowfall amounts included; 4 inches at Eagle Butte; 6 inches at Timber Lake, McLaughlin, and 14 miles north of Isabel; 7 inches at Isabel and 6 miles southeast of McIntosh; 8 inches southwest of Keldron.  More than 400 poles were lost to the heavy snow leaving approximately 800 people without power. Eighty lineman worked through the Easter weekend in the snow and mud. McLaughlin and Keldron were the hardest hit. Several hundred people were still without power on April 5th.

Very strong south winds developed over central and northeast South Dakota in the early afternoon and continued into the early evening hours. South winds of 30 to 50 mph with gusts to near 70 mph caused some structural and shingle damage across the area. In Presho, the chamber sign was blown down with a carport tipped and damaged. A pickup on Interstate-90 lost a camper to the high winds.||The high winds, combined with lowered humidity and dry fuels, helped fan several grassland fires across the region. The largest fire was started from a downed power line in Campbell County near the town of Glenham.  The fire grew to be five miles long by two miles wide and traveled eight miles before it was brought under control. Almost 6000 acres were burned with nearly 20 fire departments dispatched.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding of roads and local creeks in an area of southeast South Dakota near Interstate 90 and west of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of up to 6 inches caused widespread flash flooding of numerous roads, residences, fields, and other low areas, causing damage to and forcing evacuation of numerous residences. Many of the flooded roads were washed out. The Yankton Sioux reservation including the town of Marty was especially hard hit, with the tribal headquarters made unusable, and 63 families displaced from their homes.Flood damage was estimated at one million dollars by the county emergency manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall of 3 to 5 inches in the James River basin helped keep the James River in moderate to major flood throughout June. Agricultural land along with several roads remained flooded throughout the month.

Very heavy rainfall of 4 to 7 inches fell on the Moreau River basin which caused the Moreau river to go above the flood stage of 21 feet at Whitehorse late on June 17th.  The river quickly rose to 23.63 feet early on June 18th and then fell back below flood stage by the mid morning hours. Some agricultural land along with a few roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River, which began in March from snowmelt, continued through June with the river rising again due to heavy rain. Moderate to major flooding was observed, with the river at Mitchell rising to its highest crest for the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the James River was observed for most of the month. The river, which has been slowly falling before June, rose again due to heavy rain, and the number of fields and roads flooded increased again. Several roads in Huron were closed, and a swimming pool was closed due to the river flooding the parking lot. The river crested at near 5.7 feet above flood stage at Huron, or about 3 feet below the March crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms developed along a very strong warm front and produced nine tornadoes from Akaska to Bowdle to Hecla. The largest of the tornadoes was an EF4 tornado which occurred near Bowdle. This tornado produced devastating damage in the Bowdle area. The other tornadoes ranged from EF0 to EF2 and caused extensive tree and building damage.  Nearly one-hundred power poles were downed along with several high line towers leaving nearly a thousand customers without power. Also, very strong straight line winds and large hail up to the size of golfballs affected parts of the area causing some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado in eastern Walworth county crossed into western Edmunds county and intensified into a large EF4 tornado as it struck several farms in its path. At the first farm, several large cottonwood trees were uprooted along with damage to several trailers. Three grain bins were also destroyed with debris located several hundred yards to the northeast. The residence suffered some shingle and antenna damage. The tornado then tracked northeast to a second farm where several outbuildings were damaged or destroyed along with widespread tree damage. The main residence at this location suffered no damage. Several grain cars were also rolled about 100 yards into the trees behind the house.||The large tornado continued to track northeast to a third farm to the north of Bowdle. The main residence suffered major damage to walls with part of the roof structure removed. Widespread tree damage was sustained with many of the |trees completely debarked with only the stumps of the largest branches |remaining. Two large garages were completely destroyed with the |concrete slab wiped clean. The vehicles in one garage were |rolled or tossed from 25 to 100 yards away. It is estimated that one |vehicle flew through the air 75 to 100 yards resting in the tree |shelter belt to the north of the residence. Several other outbuildings were completely destroyed.||The tornado then toppled six to eight metal power transmission towers as it moved to the north of the farm. One tower was sheared off from the concrete footings and traveled an estimated 400 yards. Ground scouring was visible along the path of these towers.||The large tornado continued to track east crossing over State Highway 47 where a state radio tower was toppled. The tornado lifted shortly thereafter.  The highest wind speeds were estimated to be from 166 to 200 mph.

Thunderstorms produced a variety of severe weather and flash flooding in much of southeast South Dakota from late morning through the afternoon and into the early evening of June 26th. The first reports were mostly large hail and mainly north of Interstate 90, but by early evening the storms had also spread south of the interstate, and produced a greater variety of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread large hail, with numerous stones to baseball size, fell along a narrow path several miles long which included the town of De Smet. The hail heavily damaged numerous vehicles, denting vehicle bodies and smashing or cracking windows, and also heavily damaged roofs on homes and other buildings. Damage to siding and to windows on buildings was limited because the hail was accompanied by light winds. The hail damaged lights at the Laura Ingalls Wilder Pageant site. One large hailstone punched a hole in the roof of a metal building. Crop damage was suspected in the area, but the amount of crop damage was not known.

Nearly one-hundred power poles were downed along with several high line towers leaving nearly a thousand customers without power. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado entered southeast Mcpherson county from northeast Edmunds county.  The tornado struck a farm along State Highway 45 where a calving shed was completely destroyed with large sections of the roof blown over 100 yards. The tornado then caused moderate damage to a barn with one collapsed wall. Multiple softwood and hardwood trees were uprooted and many power poles were completely snapped near the base. The tornado tore the roof off a turkey barn at the Long Lake Colony. Several Wetonka homes also sustained minor roof damage. The wind speeds were estimated between 111 and 120 mph. The tornado then moved into western Brown county and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from widespread heavy rain caused minor flooding of the Missouri River near Chamberlain and also in a bend of the river from Springfield Southwest and west during mid and late June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused the Missouri River to rise slowly above flood stage. Lowlands flooded included some camping and other recreational facilities. The river crested at 3.38 feet above flood stage on June 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds over a large part of southeast South Dakota beginning just before midnight on June 10th, and continuing well into the predawn hours of June 11th. The storms also produced very heavy rain, which caused flash flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread damage in the Sioux Falls area. Wood and siding were blown off a new house and a nearby fence was blown over. The winds caused tree damage, including 2 to 3 foot diameter trees blown down. Debris from the tree damage blocked several roads. Garages were blown off three homes which were next to each other, and other nearby homes suffered significant damage in an area on West Eli Court which was subjected to the strongest winds, estimated at 100 mph. Windows were blown out in several of these homes, and a large camper was overturned in the same area. A wind gust of 74 mph was measured elsewhere in the city. The winds blew down out power lines in parts of the city.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighty mph winds southwest of Selby destroyed a 60 by 120 foot pole barn and took off the top half of a silo. Two grain bins were also blown away along with several trees uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River, which began in March from snowmelt, continued through June with the river rising again due to heavy rain. Moderate to major flooding was observed, with the river at Mitchell rising to its highest crest for the year.

The river, which has been slowly falling before June, rose again due to heavy rain, and the number of fields and roads flooded increased again. The river crested at near 6.5 feet above flood stage at Forestburg, which was a little more then half a foot below the March crest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The third tornado formed about four miles west of Bowdle on Highway 12. The tornado came down on a farm or formed immediately to the south of the farm. Several outbuildings were severely damaged with the residence sustaining siding and roof damage. A stock trailer was thrown about 75 yards from its initial point. Tree damage was sustained on the west side of the property with fallen trees leaning west. On the east side of the property, debris was scattered to the northeast. The damage on the farm was consistent with an EF2 rating with wind speeds ranging from 111 to 135 mph. The tornado continued moving northeast crossing into western Edmunds county where it intensified to an EF4.

The East Fork Vermillion River crested at 1 feet above flood stage on June 15th, and the west Fork crested at a little more than 2 feet above flood stage on June 11th, both near Parker.

A United States record setting hailstone fell from a very strong supercell thunderstorm moving southeast across central South Dakota.  The record setting hailstone fell near Vivian, South Dakota and measured 8 inches in diameter, 18.625 inches in circumference, and weighed 1.9375 pounds. This hailstone broke the previous United States record for diameter (7.0 inches - 22 June 2003 in Aurora, NE) and weight (1.67 pounds - 3 September 1970 in Coffeyville, KS).  The Aurora, Nebraska hailstone will retain the record for circumference (18.75 inches).  Several other stones of 6 inches or more in diameter were measured during the storm survey.||Along with the very large hail, damaging winds in excess of 70 mph along with an isolated tornado occurred. The large hail and high winds caused extensive damage to homes, outbuildings, and vehicles as it moved southeast across the region. Some of the hail went completely through car windshields, roofs, garages, and campers. The hail caused five minor injuries to motorists on Interstate-90 as it went through their windshields. A minor was severely injured when the glass in the mini-van he was traveling was completely shattered by the large hail. The child suffered numerous cuts, many requiring stitches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding at scattered locations in southeast South Dakota during the late evening of July 29th into the early morning predawn hours of July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 to 6 inches caused widespread flooding of roads and basements, especially in an area from the town of Howard to 4 miles north of Howard. Several county roads were washed out and some bridges were damaged. A golf course on the east side of Howard was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A United States record setting hailstone fell from a very strong supercell thunderstorm moving southeast across central South Dakota.  The record setting hailstone fell near Vivian, South Dakota and measured 8 inches in diameter, 18.625 inches in circumference, and weighed 1.9375 pounds. This hailstone broke the previous United States record for diameter (7.0 inches - 22 June 2003 in Aurora, NE) and weight (1.67 pounds - 3 September 1970 in Coffeyville, KS).  The Aurora, Nebraska hailstone will retain the record for circumference (18.75 inches).  Several other stones of 6 inches or more in diameter were measured during the storm survey.||Along with the very large hail, damaging winds in excess of 70 mph along with an isolated tornado occurred. The large hail and high winds caused extensive damage to homes, outbuildings, and vehicles as it moved southeast across the region. Some of the hail went completely through car windshields, roofs, garages, and campers. The hail caused five minor injuries to motorists on Interstate-90 as it went through their windshields. A minor was severely injured when the glass in the mini-van he was traveling was completely shattered by the large hail. The child suffered numerous cuts, many requiring stitches.

A strong upper level disturbance produced heavy rain around Lead, Deadwood, and Central City. Four to five inches of rain fell in two hours, causing widespread street flooding in Lead and Deadwood. Six to 12 inches of water flowing down Main and Sherman Streets in Deadwood flooded cars, basements, and businesses. The electrical system of the Broadway Parking Ramp elevator was destroyed. Hail broke windows of a basement pub, which then filled with water about three and a half feet deep.

Severe storms moved from Montana southeastward across western South Dakota into south central South Dakota, merging with another area of storms over west central South Dakota. The storms produced a wide swath of hail and strong winds from northern Butte County, through southern Meade, eastern Pennington, Jackson, and Bennett Counties. Millions of dollars in crop damage was reported, along with some damage to homes and automobiles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail broke the windows of a pickup and injured a small child.

Flooding of the James River, which began in march from snowmelt, continued through July as periodic heavy rains continued. The river was generally rising at the end of the month, but did not rise above earlier peak crests for the year at any forecast point. Moderate to major flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding along the James River continued, with numerous fields and some roads remaining covered. The river rose near the end of the month, when some of the heaviest rain fell, but remained below its peak March crest at Huron.

Several other stones of 6 inches or more in diameter were measured during the storm survey.||Along with the very large hail, damaging winds in excess of 70 mph along with an isolated tornado occurred. The large hail and high winds caused extensive damage to homes, outbuildings, and vehicles as it moved southeast across the region. Some of the hail went completely through car windshields, roofs, garages, and campers. The hail caused five minor injuries to motorists on Interstate-90 as it went through their windshields. A minor was severely injured when the glass in the mini-van he was traveling was completely shattered by the large hail. The child suffered numerous cuts, many requiring stitches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the Vermillion River, the East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers, and the Little Vermillion River, caused by late July heavy rains, continued through early August, ending by the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the Vermillion River, as well as the East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers, triggered by late July heavy rains, continued into early August before abating. Lowlands, fields, and some roads were flooded. The Vermillion River crested at 4.6 feet above flood stage near Davis on August 7th, then water levels steadily decreased, with the river falling below flood stage on August 12th. The East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers were already falling at the start of the month and fell below flood stage a few days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the Vermillion River, the East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers, and the Little Vermillion River, caused by late July heavy rains, continued through early August, ending by the middle of the month.

Flooding of fields, lowlands, and parks continued, then steadily abated. The most extensive flooding, reported in the Montrose area, peaked at the start of the month. Parks and campgrounds in this area were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system deepened over northern Minnesota, producing a tight pressure gradient across South Dakota and prolonged period of strong northwest winds from the morning of the 26th and continuing through the afternoon of the 27th. Sustained winds of 30 to 50 mph, with gusts to 70 mph, were recorded across the South Dakota plains. Some trees were blown down and other minor damage was reported.  Light snow across northwestern South Dakota caused blowing snow and reduced visibilities.

Heavy rain, combined with saturated ground from a summer of record rainfall, caused moderate to major flooding of the Big Sioux River beginning on September 22nd, and continuing to the end of the month. Flooding of numerous roads much farmland, and some homes was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain, combined with saturated ground, caused a fairly rapid rise of the Big Sioux River to moderate to major flooding levels. Roads and numerous agricultural land near the river were flooded, as well as a few homes near the southern edge of the county, where the flooding was worse than further north. The river crested at 4.67 feet above flood stage at Brookings on September 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of several inches caused flash flooding of numerous streets, basements, fields, and other low areas in and near Brookings. Widespread flash flooding from the overflow of Six Mile Creek was noted in the western and northwestern parts of the city. People in at least 50 houses, mobile homes, and apartments were forced to evacuate, and there was widespread water damage to structures, contents, and vehicles in this area. The widespread flooding from the creek came close to but did not reach the downtown area, although street flooding there was noted.

An unusually large and strong low pressure system with record low pressures at its center moved just east to north of the area on October 26th and moved only slowly away by late on October 27th. Widespread strong winds near this low reached occasional sustained speed above 40 mph in southeast South Dakota with gusts near or above 60 mph. The strong winds began on the morning of October 26, and with only a slight decrease that night, continued through the daytime of October 27th before decreasing more significantly that evening. Along with numerous reports of measured high winds, scattered damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged high winds from the west to northwest were caused by a very large and strong low pressure system that deepened to a record low central pressure as it moved north and northeast of the area. Sustained winds frequently reached speeds of 40 mph and there were frequent gusts to around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record breaking surface low pressure area moved across the Northern Plains and brought high winds to all of central and northeast South Dakota from the early morning of the 26th into the early evening of the 27th. Sustained northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 to 75 mph caused scattered property damage across the region along with blowing several vehicles off the road.  Along with the high winds came snowfall of 1 to 5 inches which resulted in treacherous driving conditions.  Several schools started late on the 27th due to the slippery roads and high winds.||The high winds, combined with slippery roads at times, blew several semis and other vehicles off the road on Interstate-29 and at several other locations across the region. Only minor injuries occurred with these incidents. The high winds damaged many traffic signs and signals, downed many power lines and poles, along with downing branches and several trees. As a result, several hundred customers were without power for a time across the area. The high winds caused roof and siding damage to many buildings along with damaging some fences.  A shed was also completely destroyed near Sisseton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain, combined with saturated ground, caused a fairly rapid rise of the Big Sioux River to moderate to major flooding levels. A line of thunderstorms brought damaging wind gusts up to 70 mph to parts of northeast South Dakota. Several trees were uprooted along with several structures sustaining some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventy mph winds blew a semi off of Highway 212. The semi suffered extensive damage with the driver receiving minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the James River to rise above flood stage beginning on September 18th, but the flooding was minor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding along the James River, with mainly unfarmed lowlands  affected. The river at Huron crested at less than a foot above flood stage on September 21st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding along the James River, with mainly unfarmed lowlands  affected. The river at Forestburg crested at a little less than a foot above flood stage on September 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms brought damaging wind gusts up to 70 mph to parts of northeast South Dakota. Several trees were uprooted along with several structures sustaining some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventy mph winds brought down some trees and several branches in Clear Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The James River receded and went below flood stage at both Ashton and Columbia in August. The river remained above flood stage at Stratford throughout the month. Ashton went below flood stage on the 14th while Columbia went below flood stage on the 25th. A few roads along with some agricultural land remained flooded.

Heavy rainfall caused minor to moderate flooding of the Vermillion, East and West Fork Vermillion, and the Little Vermillion Rivers beginning on September 23rd. The flooding ended before the end of the month except on the lower part of the river near Wakonda.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding of the East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers and the Little Vermillion River, with lowlands including some farmland affected. The Little Vermillion River crested at 1.2 feet above flood stage near Salem on September 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record breaking surface low pressure area moved across the Northern Plains and brought high winds to all of central and northeast South Dakota from the early morning of the 26th into the early evening of the 27th. Sustained northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 to 75 mph caused scattered property damage across the region along with blowing several vehicles off the road.  Along with the high winds came snowfall of 1 to 5 inches which resulted in treacherous driving conditions.  Several schools started late on the 27th due to the slippery roads and high winds.||The high winds, combined with slippery roads at times, blew several semis and other vehicles off the road on Interstate-29 and at several other locations across the region. Only minor injuries occurred with these incidents. The high winds damaged many traffic signs and signals, downed many power lines and poles, along with downing branches and several trees. As a result, several hundred customers were without power for a time across the area. The high winds caused roof and siding damage to many buildings along with damaging some fences.  A shed was also completely destroyed near Sisseton.

Persistent thunderstorms developed in the late morning over southeast South Dakota and continued through the afternoon and evening. All of the storms through early afternoon produced large hail, with one report of damaging wind gusts. Large hail, heavy rain and flash flooding were noted during the evening. Some of the flash flooding continued through the night and next day as flooding.

An unusually large and strong low pressure system with record low pressures at its center moved just east to north of the area on October 26th and moved only slowly away by late on October 27th. Widespread strong winds near this low reached occasional sustained speed above 40 mph in southeast South Dakota with gusts near or above 60 mph. The strong winds began on the morning of October 26, and with only a slight decrease that night, continued through the daytime of October 27th before decreasing more significantly that evening. Along with numerous reports of measured high winds, scattered damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged high winds from the west to northwest were caused by a very large and strong low pressure system that deepened to a record low central pressure as it moved north and northeast of the area. Sustained winds frequently reached speeds of 40 mph and there were frequent gusts to around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system deepened over northern Minnesota, producing a tight pressure gradient across South Dakota and prolonged period of strong northwest winds from the morning of the 26th and continuing through the afternoon of the 27th. Sustained winds of 30 to 50 mph, with gusts to 70 mph, were recorded across the South Dakota plains. Some trees were blown down and other minor damage was reported.  Light snow across northwestern South Dakota caused blowing snow and reduced visibilities.

A strong low pressure system moved across the region, bringing heavy rain to the northern Black Hills. Three to five inches of rain fell from the Cheyenne Crossing area to Lead and Deadwood during the overnight hours. Creeks rose and some minor street flooding was reported in Lead and Deadwood. A portion of a retaining wall on the west side of Lead collapsed and damaged two parked cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record breaking surface low pressure area moved across the Northern Plains and brought high winds to all of central and northeast South Dakota from the early morning of the 26th into the early evening of the 27th. Sustained northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 to 75 mph caused scattered property damage across the region along with blowing several vehicles off the road.  Along with the high winds came snowfall of 1 to 5 inches which resulted in treacherous driving conditions.  Several schools started late on the 27th due to the slippery roads and high winds.||The high winds, combined with slippery roads at times, blew several semis and other vehicles off the road on Interstate-29 and at several other locations across the region. Only minor injuries occurred with these incidents. The high winds damaged many traffic signs and signals, downed many power lines and poles, along with downing branches and several trees. As a result, several hundred customers were without power for a time across the area. The high winds caused roof and siding damage to many buildings along with damaging some fences.  A shed was also completely destroyed near Sisseton.

An unusually large and strong low pressure system with record low pressures at its center moved just east to north of the area on October 26th and moved only slowly away by late on October 27th. Widespread strong winds near this low reached occasional sustained speed above 40 mph in southeast South Dakota with gusts near or above 60 mph. The strong winds began on the morning of October 26, and with only a slight decrease that night, continued through the daytime of October 27th before decreasing more significantly that evening. Along with numerous reports of measured high winds, scattered damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged high winds from the west to northwest were caused by a very large and strong low pressure system that deepened to a record low central pressure as it moved north and northeast of the area. Sustained winds frequently reached speeds of 40 mph and there were frequent gusts to around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds at several locations in southeast South Dakota, mostly near Interstate 90, during the late afternoon and early evening of July 10th. The storms also produced heavy rain and flash flooding in Mitchell, and in the Platte area of Charles Mix County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail caused an unknown amount of crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainfall caused widespread long term flooding of roads, fields and other low areas in much of Sanborn County, including the town of Woonsocket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rain caused lasting flooding of numerous fields and other lowlands, and of many roads. Several homes were flooded and many of the flooded roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recurring heavy rainfall caused widespread long term flooding of roads, fields and other low areas in much of Hutchinson County, especially including the town of Parkston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rain caused lasting flooding of numerous fields and other lowlands, and of many roads. Several homes were flooded and many of the flooded roads were washed out or otherwise closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind gusts over 70 mph in south central Meade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 25 power poles and trees were blown down near the intersection of Elk Vale and Elk Creek Roads. A silo and other outbuildings were toppled just north of the intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail quarter size from the city of Custer to Custer State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient behind an exiting low pressure system produced strong westerly winds across far northwestern South Dakota. Sustained winds of 30 to 50 mph and gusts to 65 mph were recorded from Buffalo to the Lemmon area.

Severe storms moved from Montana southeastward across western South Dakota into south central South Dakota, merging with another area of storms over west central South Dakota. The storms produced a wide swath of hail and strong winds from northern Butte County, through southern Meade, eastern Pennington, Jackson, and Bennett Counties. Millions of dollars in crop damage was reported, along with some damage to homes and automobiles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 70 mph.

Several bands of thunderstorms during the night produced heavy rain over the southern Black Hills. Runoff from the rain caused flooding in Wind Cave National Park and along Lame Johnny and Beaver Creeks east of the Black Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over Custer County Road 101 between Highways 385 and 79.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several bands of thunderstorms during the night produced heavy rain over the southern Black Hills. Runoff from the rain caused flooding in Wind Cave National Park and along Lame Johnny and Beaver Creeks east of the Black Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek flowed over Beaver Creek Road. The river gauge four miles south of Buffalo Gap crested at 7.54 feet, above its flood stage of 7.5 feet, at 945 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed over eastern Custer and eastern Pennington Counties, then moved east-southeast across southern South Dakota. The storms produced large hail, wind gusts around 100 mph, and a weak tornado in Wall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of hail to quarter size covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused moderate to record flooding of the Vermillion River beginning on July 21st, and continuing to the end of the month. The flooding also affected the Little Vermillion River, and the East and West fork Vermillion Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused major flooding of the east and West Fork Vermillion Rivers, and the Little Vermillion River. Fields, lowlands, roads, and parks were flooded. the most extensive flooding in the county was reported in and near Montrose, where roads, basements, ball parks, a campground, and other low areas were flooded. Flooding threatened a sewer lift station in Montrose, but it was kept operational.

A tornadic thunderstorm developed over central Ziebach County and slowly moved northeastward toward Red Elm and Dupree. The storm lingered and regenerated over the Dupree area for more than three hours; producing large hail, wind gusts near 100 mph, sixteen tornadoes, and torrential rainfall that caused widespread flooding. Significant damage occurred in Dupree, where several mobile homes were blown over, roofs were damaged, and trees were downed. Two people were injured in Dupree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 60 mph were accompanied by golf ball size hail.

The storms then moved east of the Huron area to Brookings County during the late evening to just after midnight, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball size hail caused an unknown amount of damage to corn and other crops in the area. The hail also damaged vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced large hail across northern Fall River County and caused damage around Hot Springs.

A severe thunderstorm produced large hail and strong wind gusts south of the Badlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail and gusty winds broke windows of houses and produced hail drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a variety of severe weather and flash flooding in much of southeast South Dakota from late morning through the afternoon and into the early evening of June 26th. The first reports were mostly large hail and mainly north of Interstate 90, but by early evening the storms had also spread south of the interstate, and produced a greater variety of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail at the state fairgrounds was mostly pea to marble size, but a few stones as large as golf balls fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved east-southeast from northeastern Wyoming across southern Butte and southwestern Meade Counties. Large hail and strong wind gusts caused extensive damage before merging with a cluster of thunderstorms over Meade County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to tennis ball size along with some isolated flash flooding in central and northeast South Dakota.

Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon and early evening of July 17th. The storms developed initially over a long distance from Gregory to the Brookings area. The storms quickly weakened except for the storms near the eastern border of the state, which remained severe for several hours and eventually moved east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage at Oakwood Lakes State Park. A few vehicles were damaged by tree debris, a boat landing was damaged, and a camper was overturned, resulting in minor injuries.

Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds at several locations in southeast South Dakota, mostly near Interstate 90, during the late afternoon and early evening of July 10th. The storms also produced heavy rain and flash flooding in Mitchell, and in the Platte area of Charles Mix County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, measured at golf ball size, caused an unknown amount of damage to vehicles and crops. Most of the suspected crop damage was to corn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple cold air funnel clouds were reported near Clear Lake.

A severe thunderstorm moved northeastward from Nebraska across south central South Dakota, tracking from south of Martin to the Mission area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magpie Creek RAWS measured a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts caused damage on ranches in the Howes area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds ripped the roof off a large machine shed, smashed calf shelters, and tossed large round hay bales. The skirting and deck of a mobile home was torn off. At another ranch, two cone-shaped grain bins on posts were blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail quarter size from the city of Custer to Custer State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping-pong ball sized hail damaged vehicles in downtown Custer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed over eastern Custer and eastern Pennington Counties, then moved east-southeast across southern South Dakota. The storms produced large hail, wind gusts around 100 mph, and a weak tornado in Wall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball sized hail caused minor damage to automobiles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved northeastward from western Nebraska across south central South Dakota. Large hail and strong winds accompanied the storms over southeastern Bennett, Todd, and Tripp Counties. Trees were downed throughout the area; with some damage to homes, buildings, and personal property.

Thunderstorms produced damaging winds over a large part of southeast South Dakota beginning just before midnight on June 10th, and continuing well into the predawn hours of June 11th. The storms also produced very heavy rain, which caused flash flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew empty grain bins off anchors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at a few places west of the James River, then produced heavy rain and flash flooding along a path near Interstate 90 from Mitchell to Sioux Falls. The storms moved across the area during the evening of July 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of several streets in Vermillion, resulting in several stalled vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a variety of severe weather and flash flooding in much of southeast South Dakota from late morning through the afternoon and into the early evening of June 26th. The first reports were mostly large hail and mainly north of Interstate 90, but by early evening the storms had also spread south of the interstate, and produced a greater variety of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread street flooding in the city of Brookings. Water on numerous roads was high enough to block the roads, with the water quite deep on several underpasses. Yards, several basements, fields, parks, and part of a golf course were also flooded. .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved from Montana southeastward across western South Dakota into south central South Dakota, merging with another area of storms over west central South Dakota. The storms produced a wide swath of hail and strong winds from northern Butte County, through southern Meade, eastern Pennington, Jackson, and Bennett Counties. Millions of dollars in crop damage was reported, along with some damage to homes and automobiles.

A second severe thunderstorm clipped northern Ziebach County, producing large hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and strong winds broke windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts around 60 mph accompanied a storm across southern Harding County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at a few places west of the James River, then produced heavy rain and flash flooding along a path near Interstate 90 from Mitchell to Sioux Falls. The storms moved across the area during the evening of July 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding of streets in and near the town of Freeman. The water was deep enough on streets to result in numerous stalled vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and brief tornadoes in southeast South Dakota during the evening of July 23rd. The storms moved east and affected the area mainly near and south of Interstate 90. However, the storms affected an area further north, including Brookings, when they arrived near the eastern border of South Dakota. Flash flooding resulted in the Brookings area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds moved a house off its foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal thunderstorms produced hail to the size of golf balls and 60 mph wind gusts across Harding County.
A severe thunderstorm developed over Mellette County and tracked eastward across Tripp County. The storm produced hail to quarter size and wind gusts around 60 to 70 mph.

Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds at several locations in southeast South Dakota, mostly near Interstate 90, during the late afternoon and early evening of July 10th. The storms also produced heavy rain and flash flooding in Mitchell, and in the Platte area of Charles Mix County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several branches, some up to 5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to tennis ball size along with some isolated flash flooding in central and northeast South Dakota.

Flooding of the James River, which began in March from snowmelt, continued in August, but ended during the month over part of the river. Heavy rain kept river levels generally steady for a while in early August, but otherwise water levels on the river slowly fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding along the James River continued into the start of August. The river was at its highest levels during the first few days of the month, although it remained below its peak spring levels. The river then fell slowly. Flooding of fields and some roads slowly abated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail at several locations between Huron and Sioux Falls during the late afternoon and evening of July 27th. the storms also produced flash flooding in the Sioux Falls area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of streets in the southeast part of Sioux Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain along and near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon of August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent thunderstorms developed in the late morning over southeast South Dakota and continued through the afternoon and evening. All of the storms through early afternoon produced large hail, with one report of damaging wind gusts. Large hail, heavy rain and flash flooding were noted during the evening. Some of the flash flooding continued through the night and next day as flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail dented vehicles and cracked windshields. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail to quarter size fell from a thunderstorm over eastern Custer and central Pennington Counties.
A line of severe thunderstorms produced wind gusts around 60 mph and large hail across west central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail and wind gusts to 70 mph across Butte County, along U.S. Highway 212 in northern Meade County, and northeastward over northern Ziebach County.
Severe thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota, producing large hail and strong wind gusts across southern Tripp County. The hail caused damage in Colome.
A severe thunderstorm moved across Perkins County, producing large hail and strong wind gusts.
A line of severe thunderstorms moved from Wyoming across western South Dakota during the evening. The storms produced wind gusts around 70 mph, with hail falling from the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over Shannon County and tracked northward across eastern Custer, eastern Pennington, eastern Meade, and western Ziebach Counties, producing large hail and strong wind gusts.

Several supercell thunderstorms developed along a very strong warm front and produced nine tornadoes from Akaska to Bowdle to Hecla. The largest of the tornadoes was an EF4 tornado which occurred near Bowdle. This tornado produced devastating damage in the Bowdle area. The other tornadoes ranged from EF0 to EF2 and caused extensive tree and building damage.  Nearly one-hundred power poles were downed along with several high line towers leaving nearly a thousand customers without power. Also, very strong straight line winds and large hail up to the size of golfballs affected parts of the area causing some damage.

A strong surface low pressure area lifting northward across the western plains along with a strong warm front surging northward brought many severe thunderstorms to the region. Large hail up to the size of golfballs along with damaging winds up to 80 to 90 mph affected parts of central and north central South Dakota.
A couple thunderstorms brought damaging winds to 60 to 70 mph in central South Dakota. A calving shed was flipped along with some roof damage to a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated southwest of Keldron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved east-southeast from northeastern Wyoming across southern Butte and southwestern Meade Counties. Large hail and strong wind gusts caused extensive damage before merging with a cluster of thunderstorms over Meade County.

Nearly one-hundred power poles were downed along with several high line towers leaving nearly a thousand customers without power. Also, very strong straight line winds and large hail up to the size of golfballs affected parts of the area causing some damage.

The largest of the tornadoes was an EF4 tornado which occurred near Bowdle. This tornado produced devastating damage in the Bowdle area. The other tornadoes ranged from EF0 to EF2 and caused extensive tree and building damage.  Nearly one-hundred power poles were downed along with several high line towers leaving nearly a thousand customers without power. Spring snowmelt runoff, above normal May precipitation along with above normal June rainfall of 3 to 5 inches kept many roads flooded along with many acres of crop and pastureland. High water levels on area lakes also continued to be an issue affecting properties especially in Day and Marshall counties throughout June.
Severe thunderstorms developed across southwestern South Dakota and tracked eastward into south central South Dakota. These storms produced wind gusts to 70 mph, large hail, and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 60 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed over far northeastern Wyoming and tracked eastward across the northern Black Hills and the west central South Dakota plains. These storms produced hail to the size of golf balls, wind gusts to 70 mph, and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of half dollars fell over the central Black Hills.
Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding at scattered locations in southeast South Dakota during the late evening of July 29th into the early morning predawn hours of July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured rainfall of 3.38 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind gusts to 60 mph and penny sized hail near Camp Crook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts damaged a fence and blew down many tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms across southeast Hand County brought very heavy rains of 5 to 10 inches which caused the Rosehill Dam to fail resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains of 5 to 10 inches caused the Rosehill Dam to break in the early morning hours in southeast Hand county. Flash flooding began around 1 am CDT with two campers being swept up around 130 am CDT. The two campers clung to a tree until they could be rescued around 6 am CDT. They were both injured with one camper being treated for hypothermia and other airlifted to Sioux Falls to be treated for broken ribs and head trauma. ||The dam broke at 330 am CDT with the spillway breaking at 420 am CDT. There was a 100 to 150 foot hole left by the dam break. The flash flooding continued downstream on Sand Creek causing damage to area farms, filling basements, and flooding many roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The James River receded and went below flood stage at both Ashton and Columbia in August. The river remained above flood stage at Stratford throughout the month. Ashton went below flood stage on the 14th while Columbia went below flood stage on the 25th. A few roads along with some agricultural land remained flooded.
A severe thunderstorm brought large hail to the northeast of Altamont in Deuel county.
A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and moved into southwestern Meade County, producing quarter size hail in Black Hawk.

Heavy rain, combined with saturated ground from a summer of record rainfall, caused moderate to major flooding of the Big Sioux River beginning on September 22nd, and continuing to the end of the month. Flooding of numerous roads much farmland, and some homes was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain, combined with saturated ground, caused a fairly rapid rise of the Big Sioux River to moderate to major flooding levels. Roads and numerous agricultural land near the river were flooded, as well as a few homes. A dike break just south of Big Springs caused additional farmland to be flooded. The river crested at 6.9 feet above flood stage near Akron, Iowa on September 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at several locations along a long path from Gregory to Brookings Counties in southeast South Dakota during the evening of August 19th. There was also one report of marginally large hail.
A strong warm front brought many severe thunderstorms with large hail up to the size of golfballs along with very heavy rains which caused flash flooding.

Thunderstorms produced large hail and a few tornadoes in southeast South Dakota west of the James River during the afternoon and early evening of July 6th. The storms then moved east of the Huron area to Brookings County during the late evening to just after midnight, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain wrapped tornado caused no reported damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms brought damaging wind gusts up to 70 mph to parts of northeast South Dakota. Several trees were uprooted along with several structures sustaining some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventy mph winds blew out a window in the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms moving southeast across the region brought very large hail up to softball size along with damaging winds to parts of northeast South Dakota. Numerous homes, vehicles, along with thousands of acres of crops were damaged.

Thunderstorms produced a variety of severe weather and flash flooding in much of southeast South Dakota from late morning through the afternoon and into the early evening of June 26th. The first reports were mostly large hail and mainly north of Interstate 90, but by early evening the storms had also spread south of the interstate, and produced a greater variety of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds rolled over a motor home at the intersection of Interstates 29 and 90. Four people were trapped in the motor home, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds over a large part of southeast South Dakota beginning just before midnight on June 10th, and continuing well into the predawn hours of June 11th. The storms also produced very heavy rain, which caused flash flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of fields, roads, and at least one basement in a rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds over a large part of southeast South Dakota beginning just before midnight on June 10th, and continuing well into the predawn hours of June 11th. The storms also produced very heavy rain, which caused flash flooding at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including up to 6 inch diameter branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and brief tornadoes in southeast South Dakota during the evening of July 23rd. The storms moved east and affected the area mainly near and south of Interstate 90. However, the storms affected an area further north, including Brookings, when they arrived near the eastern border of South Dakota. Flash flooding resulted in the Brookings area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including large trees blown down. One healthy maple tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor snowmelt flooding occurred for a short time on the Grand River in eastern Corson county. The river at Little Eagle rose above the flood stage of 15 feet to 15.64 feet before falling back below flood stage on March 24th.
